rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical [2+1] cyclopropanation (with diazo compounds) and [2+2] cycloaddition (with styrenes) of alkenes proceeding via anodically generated alkene radical cations
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich alkene to an alkene radical cation; subsequent cathodic reduction of a downstream radical cation closes the cycle (paired electrolysis in undivided cell)

key_intermediates.name: alkene radical cation B (from anodic oxidation of styrene/anethole A)
distonic radical cation D from addition of diazo compound C to B (retains N2)
1,3-distonic radical cation E after N2 extrusion (precursor to cyclopropane)
cyclopropane product F (after cathodic reduction of E)
acyclic radical cation H from addition of styrene G to B ([2+2] pathway)
cyclobutane product I (after cathodic reduction of H)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the electron-rich alkene A to radical cation B; B is intercepted by a nucleophile (diazo compound for [2+1], styrene for [2+2]) to give a new radical cation (D→E after N2 loss, or H); cathodic reduction then delivers the neutral cyclopropane F or cyclobutane I.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Complete suppression of both [2+1] and [2+2] reactions (0% yield) when added at the start, supporting a radical (radical cation) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: E- vs Z-stilbene (cis/trans olefin probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Both E- and Z-stilbene give exclusively trans-cyclopropane (E: 82%, Z: 67% yield), consistent with a freely rotating open-chain radical cation intermediate rather than concerted cycloaddition; trans-olefin reacts faster

probe_reagents.name: Divided (H-cell) vs undivided cell
probe_reagents.observation: [2+1] drops to <10% in H-cell (vs 79% undivided) — requires cathodic reduction step; [2+2] gives 19% in H-cell (vs 78% undivided), suggesting a partial radical-chain contribution for [2+2]

notes: Stereochemical scrambling (E/Z → same trans-cyclopropane) is taken as evidence for a stepwise radical-cation mechanism rather than a concerted [2+1]/[2+2]. Authors explicitly do not exclude a radical-chain pathway in which radical cations E/H are reduced by another molecule of alkene A instead of at the cathode; the chain contribution appears more significant for the [2+2] (H-cell yield 19%) than for the [2+1] (H-cell yield <10%). A minor proton-migrated (ring-opened α,β-unsaturated ester) byproduct (<6%) is observed in the [2+1] reaction, consistent with deprotonation of the 1,3-distonic radical cation E competing with cyclization/reduction.
